name: Qutham ibn Abbas
title: Qutham ibn Abbas (RA): The Prophet's Likeness
meta_description: The story of Qutham ibn Abbas (RA), the youngest son of Abbas and the child who most resembled the Prophet Muhammad. The last to rise from his grave, a man of quiet integrity who carried the banner of his family to the far edge of the world.
keywords: Qutham ibn Abbas, story of Qutham, sons of Abbas, family of the Prophet, resembled the Prophet, Banu Hashim, sahaba stories
lede: He was the baby of a famous house. His father was beloved, his brothers were leaders and scholars, and at every turn there was someone larger ahead of him. It would have been easy to be lost in such a family.

This is the story of Qutham ibn Abbas (may Allah be pleased with him): the youngest son of Abbas, the child who looked most like the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, and a man who quietly found his own place at the very edge of the Muslim world.

sections.h2: The youngest in a house of giants
paras: Qutham was the youngest child of Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the uncle of the Prophet ﷺ. To be born into that family was to begin life in the shadow of others. His father was so loved that he was almost a father to the Prophet ﷺ himself. His brothers, al-Fadl and Abdullah, were already on their way to greatness.

Think for a moment of what that must feel like. All these famous names ahead of you, each one a kind of star, and you arriving last. How does a child even begin to find his own place in a house like that?
His name was unusual even then. There is a famous poem about him, and the name Qutham is so rare that most people will go their whole lives without meeting one.

But the youngest carried something none of the others did. Of all the family, he resembled the Prophet ﷺ the most in his appearance. He was only a small child in the Prophet's lifetime, so the likeness showed itself fully later, as he grew into a man.

sections.h2: The faces that mirrored a face
paras: The companions remembered who looked like the Prophet ﷺ the way you remember something precious. Ja'far ibn Abi Talib was told, "You resemble my character and my creation," and was sent as the ambassador to Abyssinia, a perfect representation of the one he resembled.

From the family there were others. Hasan, the grandson, looked so much like him that Abu Bakr once lifted the child into the air and said he resembled his grandfather more than his own father, Ali. There was Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith, the cousin and childhood friend who looked exactly like him, whose long absence from Islam hurt the Prophet ﷺ deeply, and whose return brought him real joy.

It is worth noticing how much this likeness mattered. Two of the men who resembled the Prophet ﷺ, Ja'far and Mus'ab ibn Umayr, were sent out as his ambassadors. When Mus'ab fell in battle, some of the enemy cried out that they had killed Muhammad ﷺ, because the face they saw was so close to his.

And then there was Qutham. The scholars would debate it, and some of them said it was Qutham who was the most identical of all to the Prophet ﷺ in his appearance. It was a quiet inheritance, given to the youngest, and it shaped how his family held him.

sections.h2: A dream, and a brother through milk
paras: His mother, Lubaba, had a strange dream while she was pregnant. She saw that a part of the Prophet ﷺ was in her house. Not his whole body, only a part of him, and she could not say which. She came to him and asked what it meant.

He answered gently. She would give birth to a child, he said, and she would nurse another child alongside her own son. That other child was al-Husayn, the grandson of the Prophet ﷺ. So Qutham and al-Husayn grew up as brothers through nursing, raised side by side.

It is moving to think of the timing. This was the last stretch of the Prophet's life, after the move to Madinah, when his family began to gather close around him again. You can sense how much joy that gave him: to have his people near, to build a full family life in his final months, to hold the babies of his household in his arms.

There is a tender scene from those days. Lubaba was nursing al-Husayn when the Prophet ﷺ took the baby and cuddled him, and the child, being a baby, urinated on him. The Prophet ﷺ asked her to hold his son so he could clean himself. She took al-Husayn back, but then she smacked the baby and he began to cry. The Prophet ﷺ looked at her and said, gently, that she had hurt him through his son. This is how nearness to the Prophet ﷺ reached this family: in ordinary, domestic moments of love.

Qutham knew that nearness too. He once recalled playing with his brother Abdullah when the Prophet ﷺ passed by, scooped the two boys up, and carried them along with him. The same hands that received revelation reached down to gather up two playing children.

While the Prophet ﷺ was being besieged in Madinah, his uncle Abbas was still in Makkah, and he was deeply afraid. The army gathered against the Muslims was the largest the Arabs had ever assembled, and the aim was to wipe the believers out entirely.

In his fear, Abbas would hold the little boy who looked most like the Prophet ﷺ. He would clutch Qutham close and whisper to him as if to a piece of the one he loved. The lines became a famous poem: my beloved, my beloved, the likeness of the one with the noble nose, the Prophet of the Lord of all blessings, despite the hatred of those who oppose him.

He praised the Prophet's noble nose and shamed the noses of his enemies in the dirt, holding tight to the child while he waited for news. When a servant finally came and told him that the Prophet ﷺ and the companions had survived, Abbas kissed the man on his forehead and set him free.

sections.h2: The last to leave the grave
paras: Each of the sons of Abbas was woven into the final days of the Prophet ﷺ, and Qutham was given a distinction that is hard to imagine. He was the last person to come out of the grave when the Prophet ﷺ was buried.

He had the last moments down there with the body of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. He was the one who placed the last stone over him before rising from the grave. Of all the people gathered for that burial, it was the youngest son of Abbas who lingered longest beside him.
His brothers were part of these same hours. His eldest brother, al-Fadl, was the one who took the last walk with the Prophet ﷺ. The whole family was drawn close to that moment of farewell.

It is a small detail, easily lost in the pages of history. Yet it tells you something about a quiet boy in a loud family: when the most painful and most sacred task of all was at hand, he was the one who stayed until the very end.

sections.h2: Integrity in the years that followed
paras: Qutham lived a long life, as most of the children of Abbas did. He grew into a man of courage and went far into the conquests, taking part in the opening of lands across Iraq and Persia and beyond. Later, when Ali went out to Iraq, he appointed Qutham as governor of Madinah.

He was known above all for his dignity. After one battle, a man tried to give him a thousand extra shares because of his bravery. Qutham refused. Put it back among the spoils, he said. Allocate it to the Muslims. I do not want it. He held his integrity higher than any reward.

It would have been the easiest thing in the world to take it. He had earned it on the battlefield, and the offer was made openly. But he understood that a reward is not only about what you deserve. It is about what it does to your heart, and he wanted nothing that might pull him toward himself and away from the cause he served.

His life reached its furthest point at the opening of Samarqand, far to the east. He carried into that battle the banner of Banu Hashim, the very same banner his eldest brother, al-Fadl, had carried beside the Prophet ﷺ when only a few stood firm with him. Qutham died there as a martyr, and was buried in that distant land. Generations later, the great scholar of hadith, Imam al-Bukhari, would be born in the same region. The young man who looked like the Prophet ﷺ had quietly helped carry Islam to the place that would one day guard the Prophet's words.

sections.h2: Do not only watch the superstar
paras: Abbas had a son of dominating presence and eloquence in Abdullah, the one people called the most beautiful and the most intelligent of men. It is tempting to look only at him and stop there.

But the lesson of the sons of Abbas is the opposite. Do not only watch the superstar. Look at what was being built around him. Each of the brothers found his own way, and Allah alone knows the good they did that was never written down.

From this one family came a line that stretched across centuries. The historians counted dozens of leaders descended from the children of Abbas, an empire that endured, in one form or another, for roughly a thousand years. It began with a father of great character and a house full of sons, including a quiet youngest child who looked like the Prophet ﷺ and gave everything he had.

Qutham is a reminder that history is not only made by the famous few. It is also carried by the ones beside them: the brother who stays, the soldier who refuses the extra share, the young man who takes a banner to the far edge of the world and is buried where almost no one back home will ever see his grave.

lessons_intro: His story is not built on one famous moment. It is built on a quiet faithfulness that stayed steady whether anyone was watching or not.

lessons.bold: Your place is your own to find.
lessons.text: Born the youngest among giants, Qutham did not compete for the spotlight. He grew into his own gift and his own service, in his own time.

lessons.bold: Stay until the end of what matters.
lessons.text: At the hardest task of all, the burial of the Prophet ﷺ, he was the last to leave the grave. Faithfulness often looks like simply not walking away.

lessons.bold: Refuse what you have not earned.
lessons.text: Offered a thousand extra shares for his courage, he sent them back to the Muslims. Dignity meant wanting nothing he did not deserve.
A quiet man carried his family's banner to a distant frontier, and helped bring Islam to a land that would one day guard the Prophet's words.

faq.a: He was the youngest son of Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the uncle of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, and a cousin of the Prophet. He is remembered as the family member who most resembled the Prophet in appearance, and as the last person to rise from his grave at the burial.

faq.q: Why is Qutham said to resemble the Prophet ﷺ?
faq.a: Several of the Prophet's relatives were known to look like him. Among them, some scholars held that Qutham was the most identical to the Prophet ﷺ in appearance. As he was only a child in the Prophet's lifetime, the likeness showed itself fully once he grew into a man.

faq.q: What is the famous poem connected to Qutham?
faq.a: During the siege of Madinah, his father Abbas, anxious in Makkah, would hold the young Qutham close because he looked like the Prophet ﷺ. The lines he whispered, praising the beloved likeness of the Prophet, became a well-known poem.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Qutham?
faq.a: That a quiet person in a famous family can find a place of his own, that staying faithful to the end matters more than standing in the spotlight, and that real dignity refuses what it has not earned.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us know what it is to feel small beside others: a sibling, a colleague, a name that fills the room before we enter it. Qutham's life is a gentle answer to that feeling. He did not chase the front of the family. He grew into the gift he was given, stayed beside the Prophet ﷺ until the last stone was placed, and refused a reward he felt he had not earned.

Carry one piece of that into your own day. Be the one who stays until the work is truly finished, who wants nothing that is not honestly theirs, who trusts that a quiet life given fully to Allah is never lost, even if no one writes it down.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Qutham ibn Abbas (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

Generations later, Imam al-Bukhari would be born in that same land.

**Do not only watch the superstar.**

Look at what is being built quietly around him.

social.caption:
He was the youngest son of Abbas, born into a family of leaders and scholars. It would have been easy to be lost. Yet of the whole household, Qutham ibn Abbas (may Allah be pleased with him) resembled the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ the most.

He grew up as a milk-brother to al-Husayn. At the burial of the Prophet ﷺ, he was the last to leave the grave. Years later he refused a reward he had not earned and carried his family's banner to a distant frontier, dying a martyr at the opening of Samarqand.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The child who looked most like the Prophet ﷺ
email.alt_subject: The last to leave the grave
email.preview: He was the baby of a famous family, and he found his own quiet place in it.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

He was the baby of the family. His father, Abbas, was so beloved he was almost a father to the Prophet ﷺ. His brothers were leaders and scholars. It would have been easy for the youngest to be lost among them.

This is the story of Qutham ibn Abbas (may Allah be pleased with him).

Of the whole family, Qutham resembled the Prophet ﷺ the most. Several relatives were known to look like him, but some scholars held that Qutham was the most identical of all. He was only a small child in the Prophet's lifetime, so the likeness showed itself fully as he grew.

His mother once dreamed that a part of the Prophet ﷺ was in her house. He told her it meant she would nurse his grandson al-Husayn alongside her own son, and so Qutham and al-Husayn grew up as brothers through milk. Nearness to the Prophet ﷺ reached this family in ordinary, domestic moments of love.

When the Prophet ﷺ was buried, Qutham was given a distinction that is hard to imagine. He was the last person to come out of the grave. He placed the last stone over the body of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ before rising. Of everyone gathered, the quiet youngest son was the one who stayed longest.

He lived a long life and grew into a man of dignity. After one battle, a man tried to give him a thousand extra shares for his bravery. He refused. Put it back among the spoils, he said. Give it to the Muslims. I do not want it.

He carried his family's banner to the opening of Samarqand, far to the east, and died there as a martyr. Generations later, the great scholar Imam al-Bukhari would be born in that same region.

There is a lesson in him that is easy to miss: do not only watch the superstar. Look at what is being built quietly around him. A small life, given fully to Allah, is never lost.

May Allah be pleased with Qutham, and gather us in the company of the people he loved.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: He was the baby of a famous house. His father, Abbas, was beloved, and his brothers were already leaders and scholars. To be the youngest among such giants was to begin life in the shadow of others.

Qutham ibn Abbas (may Allah be pleased with him) carried something none of the others did. Of all the family, he resembled the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ the most in his appearance. He was only a small child then, so the likeness showed itself fully as he grew into a man.

His mother once dreamed that a part of the Prophet ﷺ was in her house. He told her, gently, that it meant she would nurse his grandson al-Husayn alongside her own son. So the two boys grew up as brothers through milk, and the love of the Prophet's household became part of their ordinary days.

While Madinah was under siege and the largest army the Arabs had ever raised gathered against the believers, his father waited in Makkah, afraid. He would hold the little boy who looked like the Prophet ﷺ and whisper to him, "My beloved, my beloved." When the news finally came that the Prophet ﷺ had survived, Abbas kissed the messenger and set him free.

Years passed, and the Prophet ﷺ was laid to rest. Of everyone gathered for that burial, it was Qutham who lingered longest. He was the last to leave the grave, the one who placed the last stone over the body of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ before rising.
He grew into a man of courage and quiet dignity. When someone offered him a thousand extra shares for his bravery, he sent them back to the Muslims and kept nothing for himself.

At last he carried his family's banner to the opening of Samarqand, far to the east, and died there as a martyr. Long after, in that same land, the great scholar Imam al-Bukhari would be born. The quiet youngest son had helped carry Islam to the very place that would one day guard the Prophet's words.
storybook.pullquote: Put it back among the spoils. Give it to the Muslims. I do not want it.
storybook.reflection: It is easy to feel small beside louder names. Qutham's life answers that feeling. He did not chase the front of his family; he grew into his own gift, stayed beside the Prophet ﷺ until the last stone was placed, and refused a reward he had not earned. A quiet life given fully to Allah is never wasted, even when no one writes it down.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
